3. Factoring

We may sometimes assume rings are integral domains and elements are nonzero in this chapter.

3.1. Irreducibility and primality

Definition 14. Suppose is an integral domain.

  1. divides if there exists such that .
  2. and are associatives if and . This is equivalent to saying for some unit .
  3. A proper factorization of is where and are not units.
  4. is irreducible if it is not a unit and has no proper divisors.
  5. is a prime element if is not a unit, and implies or .

Remark. in every nonzero prime ideal is maximal, so for any prime number , the quotient is a field.

Warning: irreducibility and primality are not the same thing in general! For example, is irreducible but not prime in . In particular, but does not divide either . Thus is not prime.

Lemma 10.

  1. iff
  2. is irreducible if and only if there is not proper prinpical ideal with .
  3. Suppose is a PID. If is irreducible, then is maximal.
Proof

(1) and (2) follow from the definitions. (3) is implied by (2).

□

For example, is irreducible in but is not maximal since .

  • divides both and

Say are relatively prime if .

Remark:

  1. The gcd may not exist. For example, consider and . Both and are common divisors.
  2. If is a PID then the gcd always exists, since for some .

  2. and .
  3. If divides both and , it also divides

Proof

Proposition 13. Let be a PID, and .

  1. If are relatively prime, for some .
  2. Maximal ideals of are the principal ideals generated by irreducible elements.
  3. An element in is irreducible if and only if it is prime.
Proof
  1. Follows from last lemma.

  2. Suppose is irreducible and suppose . Then by irreducibility we must have or . We either have or .

    Suppose is maximal and is not irreducible. Then for some non-units , so is strictly larger than . We must have or . The first implies is a unit. and the latter implies is a unit, yielding a contradiction.

  3. Forward: Suppose is prime. Suppose is not irreducible, so we can write for non-units . By primality, WLOG suppose . We can write , yielding . Using the cancellation rule for integral domains, we have , implying is a unit and yielding a contradiction. Thus must be irreducible.

    Backward: Suppose is irreducible and divides but not . Then . By (2), we have is maximal. Thus must be the entire ring, i.e. for suitable choices of . Multiplying both sides by yields . The RHS is divisible by , so must be as well.

□

For integral domains we only have one direction guaranteed:

Proposition 14. Let be an integral domain.

  1. A prime element of is irreducible.
Proof

Follow proof of (3) forward. It only requires is a PID.

□

3.2. Euclidean Domains and Unique Factorization Domains

So far the notions of rings we have discussed include

  • integral domain
  • principal ideal domian

We will add two more to the list: Euclidean domain and unique factorization domain

Definition 16. A Euclidean domain is an integral domain with a size function such that the division algorithm is possible, i.e. for any , , there exist , not necessarily unique, such that with or .

Note that under this definition need not be multiplicative. Examples: , , .

Example 3. Prove that and are Euclidean domains, but that may not be.

Proof

Define the size function as . Now consider

(28)

Let where denotes round. We have . Thus we have a found a size function that works.

□

The same size function for works, but not for , since we have (draw the lattice and consider dividing by ). In particular, the step that breaks is the inequality when . (I spent a lot of time thinking about this)

You might ask why we can’t try another size function that may work, for example . But we should always be able to find a counterexample; in this case, for example consider the lattice formed by the .

Later we will prove that is not a PID so it cannot be a ED, by the following proposition. Another way is to show that is not a UFD since , and thus it cannot be a PID nor ED.

The following fact allows us to eliminate candidates for E.D.s.

Proposition 15. Suppose is a Euclidean domain. Then is a principal ideal domain.
Proof

Let be an ED, and let be an ideal. Take a minimal element with respect to the size function. Then . It suffices to show that . This follows from divison: implies , so we must have , otherwise minimality of is contradicted.

□

Definition 17. An integral domain is a unique factorization domain (UFD) if

  1. Factorization terminates, i.e. any element can be factorized into a product of irreducibles.
  2. The factorization is unique in the sense that then and and are associatives (up to reordering and multiplication by units if necessary.)

Examples

  • In , . and are associatives, and and are associatives
  • UFDs include and and (not easy to see) and (not easy to see).

Lemma 12. Let be an integral domain. The following are equivalent:

  1. Factorization terminates in .

  2. There is no infinite series of strictly increasing proper principal ideals:

    (29)

Proof

Suppose there exists whose factorization does not terminate. Then we get an infinite series of factors , implying Suppose we have an infinite series. We can use it to construct a factorization that does not terminate.

□

Proposition 16. is a PID implies is a UFD.
Proof

Let be a PID. By the previous lemma, it suffices to show there are no infinite series of strictly increasing propering principal ideals.

Suppose there is such a series. Let be the union. It is generated by one element is is a PID. Then there exists s.t. . Then , yielding a contradiction.

Now we prove uniqueness. Let for irreducibles and . is prime, meaning it divides one of . Thus and are associatives. Use the cancellation law, and repeat the process to conclude .

□

Summary: ED implies PID implies UFD.

Examples

  • is not a UFD, since . We can show that these four numbers are irreducible by considering the multiplicative norm function . In particular, no elements of norm or exist in . We can to norms to find the units and argue that is not associates with either
  • is a UFD since its a PID.
  • is not a PID but it is a UFD. We will prove this in the next section.

In Lemma 12, we showed that in a PID, primeness and irreducibility coincide. This occurs in a UFD as well for different reasons.

Proposition 17. In a UFD, primeness and irreducibility coincide.
Proof

For integral domains we have already shown that primality implies irreducibility. It suffices to show that irreducibility implies primality by above. Suppose and is irreducible. By the UFD property, must appear in the factorizations of or , implying or .

□

3.3. Factoring in

Definition 18. Let be a polynomial with positive leading coefficient and degree at least . Call primitive if the coefficients of do not share a common factor.

We have not yet made any statements on primality or irreducibility of primitive polynomials

Lemma 13.

  1. is primitive if and only if it is nonzero under the projection map , sending to , for all primes .
  2. Let . Then can uniquely be written as with and primitive.
  3. If , then
Proof
  1. By definition.
  2. Let be the product of the denominators, and multiply this by the gcd of the integer coefficients in to get . Proof of uniqueness: . We must have since both and are primitive.
  3. Follow the construction procedure and invoke uniqueness.

□

Lemma 14 (Gauss's Lemma). If are primitive. Then, so is .
Proof

The leading coefficent of is greater than zero. is nonzero, since both and are, and is an integral domain. Hence is primitive by Lemma 13.

□

Lemma 15 (Polynomial division in yields integer coefficients). Let with primitive. If divides in , say , then , and thus divides as well.
Proof

Let , and let and with , invoking (2) in Lemma 13. Since , we have . We have . By Gauss’s lemma, is primitive, so we must have and by uniqueness. Thus .

□

Corollary 1 ⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️.

  1. Let . is reducible in implies is reducible in , or equivalently, is irreducible in implies irreducible in . The converse is not necessarily true, since in but is a unit in .
  2. Let be primitive. Then is irreducible in if and only if it is irreducible in .

Corollary 2. (Rational root theorem). If is a root of a polynomial , then the primitive must divide . Use the fact that the divisor is also in .

Theorem 6.

  1. The irreducible elements in with positive leading coefficients are: prime integers and the primitive polynomials that are irreducible in .
  2. These are also prime elements of .
  3. is a UFD, i.e. any polynomial admits a unique factorization into irreducible (prime) elements.
Proof
  1. Suppose is irreducible in . Then, regarded as polynomial in , we have for some primitive polynomial and . Since is irreducible, we must have , implying that is primitive. Next by lem_QQdivision, irreducibility in implies irreducibility in . Use Corollary 1 for the backwards proof.
  2. Suppose in . Write and for integers . Then in and thus or in since is a PID (Lemma 12). By lem_QQdivision, in , showing that is prime.
  3. Let be a polynomial. Write where is primitive. We can uniquely factor since is a UFD. Viewed in (PID ⇒ UFD), we can factor into irreducibles. Reducibility in implies reducibility in , and we can invoke (1) to show that these factors are irreducible in . To prove uniqueness, let . Again, view this in . Since is a UFD, these factorizations are unique, implying the same in .

□

Theorem 7 (Polynomial ring is a UFD). More generally, if is a UFD, then is also a UFD.
Proof

Proceed by induction on the degree of . The base case is satisfied by hypothesis that is a UFD. Suppose . Suppose admits a unique factorization . We proceed in the exact same manner with and , using and it’s fraction field .

□

3.4. Gauss primes

The Gauss integers form a PID and thus a UFD. Prime elements of are called Gauss primes.

For example, 2 and 5 are not Gauss primes, but are.

Lemma 16. Let be an odd prime, and let , which has order .

  1. contains an element of order iff .
  2. has order 4 if and only if .
Proof
  1. Forward is obvious by Lagrange. Suppose . Consider the endomorphism . Then and . By Cauchy’s theorem, has an element of order if and only if
  2. Obvious

□

Lemma 17. An integer prime is either a Gauss prime or a product of a Gauss prime .
Proof

Since is a PID, we can factor where are Gauss primes. Thus is divisble by some integer in and . Thus either equals or . If , then and are associates since is a UFD. Thus is a Gauss prime. If , then the second case is achieved.

□

Theorem 8. Let be an odd prime integer. The following are equivalent:

  1. There exists a Gauss prime such that
  2. , sum of squares of .
  3. .
  4. is a square in .
Proof

is obvious. is proved by the first lemma.

. By lem_ππ, it suffices to show is a Gauss prime if and only if is not a square in . Suppose is a Gauss prime. Since is a PID this occurs only if is irreducible and thus is maximal. This occurs if and only if is an field, and . This occurs if and only if is irreducible in , which occurs if and only if is not a square in (since then we must have implying it has roots, or equivalently, )

□

3.5. Factoring Polynomials

Irreducibility in implies irreducibilty in . This can be used to for factorizing polynomials in .

Example 4. Show that and are irreducible in .
  1. . Take mod 5 to get , which has no linear or quadratic factors (check with bash, does by Sophie-Germain).
  2. . Take to conclude . Thus there is no linear factor modulo since we’d either need or divides . Thus check polynomials of degree 2. is the only irreducible polynomial of degree in , and it does not divide . Hence is irreducible.

Proposition 18 (Eisenstein Criterion). Let and be prime. Suppose

  3. .

Then is irreducible in and .

Proof

Mod , we have . Suppose . We must have and . Thus , which implies . This is a contradiction, hence is irreducible.

□

Proposition 19. The cyclotomic polynomial is irreducible in and when is prime.
Proof

We have and let . Note that is irreducible if and only if is irreducible. We get . We get

(30)

Dividing the both sides by shows that satisfies the Eisenstein criterion.

□
